blurb: The iconic nut pine of the Southwest mesas — a slow, drought-proof, rounded evergreen that, in good years, drops the sweet, rich pine nuts of pesto and tradition.
seasons: Evergreen blue-green paired needles year-round; rounded cones with edible nuts in fall of good years; reddening bark with age
wildlife: Pine nuts feed jays, nutcrackers, squirrels, and bears; dense cover for birds

shots.cap: Summer — whole rounded pinyon · © Austin Saunders (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — cones bearing pine nuts · © Sequoia Janirella Wrens (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Spring — needles & pollen cones · © dnvarga (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: Hardy and at home on the Front Range — a tough high-plains/foothills conifer that handles Boulder's 5b–6a cold and intense high-altitude sun; native across the intermountain region (incl. Colorado) on dry slopes and mesas.[1]
care.planting: Plant balled-and-burlapped or, better, container trees in spring or early fall.[1] Like other deep-rooted dry-country pines it resents root disturbance, so use small containerized stock and keep transplant shock low. Container trees dry out fast here and need watering through establishment; once rooted in it is exceptionally drought-tough.[1]
care.sun: Full sun — it is a sun-loving open-woodland tree.[2]
care.soil: Dry, rocky, well-drained soils of almost any texture from sand to clay loam; well suited to Boulder's lean alkaline ground. Good drainage matters most.[2]
care.water: One of the most drought-resistant pines, needing only 12–18 in of rainfall a year — a true xeric tree that needs no supplemental water once established on the Front Range, though it grows best on slightly wetter sites.[1]
care.spacing: Slow-growing and small — commonly 10–30 ft tall with a rounded, spreading crown — but extremely long-lived (often 400+ years); give it open room and don't crowd it.[1][2]
care.propagation: Grown from seed: sow fresh, untreated seed in late fall, or give stored seed about 6–8 weeks of cold-moist stratification (~40°F).[2] Seedlings establish best in the light shade of a nurse shrub with adequate first-summer moisture; germination of good seed runs above 80%.[1] Collect cones in late summer before they open and dry them to release the seed.[2]
care.maintenance: Very low maintenance; mainly remove dead wood. It is somewhat susceptible to wind damage and the pitchy wood is flammable, so avoid crowding it against structures.[2]
care.harvest: Edible piñon nuts ripen in fall, but good seed crops come only every 4–7 years, and trees don't bear significant quantities of seed until ~75–100 years old.[1]

references.name: USDA NRCS Plant Guide — Two-Needle Pinyon (Pinus edulis)
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Pinus edulis

edible.summary: The seeds — piñon / pine nuts — are a prized, richly flavored food eaten raw or roasted.[1]
edible.parts: The large wingless seeds (10–15 mm) held in the cones; ripe in fall. Piñon ranks first among native North American nut trees and was once a staple food of Southwestern peoples.[1]
edible.preparation: Gather cones in late summer/fall before they fully open, dry them to release the seeds, then eat the nuts raw, roasted in the shell, or candied in syrup.[1]
edible.caution: The nuts themselves are wholesome with no special toxicity. The main catch is supply: good seed crops occur only every 4–7 years, trees don't bear heavily until decades old, and birds, woodrats, bears, and deer compete hard for the harvest.[1]
